title: Coherent control of chirality in Weyl semimetals
authors: Amar Bharti, Margarita Khokhlova, Misha Ivanov, Gopal Dixit
abstract: Weyl fermions in inversion-symmetric Weyl semimetals occur in pairs of opposite chirality, leading to symmetric optical responses under circularly-polarised light and a vanishing net photocurrent. Here, we show that tailored two-colour light fields break this symmetry and enable selective excitation of individual Weyl nodes. The interference between a circularly-polarised $ω$ field and a phase-locked linearly-polarised $2ω$ field generates a chirality-dependent redistribution of carriers in momentum space, resulting in a nonzero controllable photocurrent. We demonstrate that both the magnitude and sign of the photocurrent can be tuned via the relative phase and field strength of the two colours, and identify an optimal regime in which chiral selectivity is maximised. Our results establish a general route to optically-controlled chiral charge dynamics in Weyl semimetals using polarisation-structured light.
summary: The paper demonstrates how using structured two-color light fields (combining $\omega$ CPL and $2\omega$ LP) can break the inherent symmetry of Weyl semimetals. This symmetry breaking allows for the selective excitation of opposite chirality nodes, resulting in a controllable, non-zero net photocurrent that is tunable by the relative phase and strength of the two laser colors.
main_problem: The primary goal is to achieve selective excitation of individual Weyl nodes in Weyl semimetals to generate a controllable net photocurrent, overcoming the natural cancellation from opposite chirality pairs.
main_result: A combination of circularly-polarized ($\omega$) and phase-locked linearly-polarized ($2\omega$) light fields breaks inversion symmetry, inducing a measurable, sign-tunable, and chirality-dependent net photocurrent.
method: The analysis uses the semiconductor Bloch equations solved in the Houston basis to model carrier dynamics under time-dependent electromagnetic fields.
model_or_system: The system is an inversion-symmetric Weyl semimetal described by low-energy Hamiltonians near the nodes. The dynamics are driven by a total vector potential combining CPL ($\omega$) and LP ($2\omega$) components.
key_observables: Net photocurrent ($J_{DC}$), current asymmetry ($\Delta J = J_{LCP} - J_{RCP}$), and population redistribution parameter ($\eta$).
important_parameters: Relative field strength of the $2\omega$ field ($ eta$) and its relative phase ($\phi_{2\omega}$). Optimal chiral selectivity is found in an intermediate regime ($0.2 \lesssim  eta \lesssim 1.6$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on modeling the system using low-energy Hamiltonians near the Weyl nodes and solving the quantum master equation.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate that single-color CPL light yields zero net current, while the two-color field generates a nonzero photocurrent. Specifically, Fig. 4 shows $J_{DC}$ reversing sign as $\phi_{2\omega}$ changes from $0^\circ$ to $180^\circ$, confirming chiral control.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the problem of current cancellation in Weyl semimetals and introduces the two-color field mechanism. It details the theoretical framework using Bloch equations, demonstrates how symmetry breaking leads to chirality-dependent population asymmetry ($\eta$), and quantifies this effect through a controllable net photocurrent ($J_{DC}$).
why_interesting: This work provides an explicit quantum optical control scheme for manipulating electronic chiral currents in topological materials, which is highly relevant to advanced optoelectronics and spintronics.
